Name: CurvatureAwareAdaptiveRouting
Title: Curvature-Aware Adaptive Mixed-Precision Routing for Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging curvature diagnostics and spectral quantization, we can develop a training-free, adaptive mixed-precision routing framework that dynamically adjusts precision based on the local geometry of the loss landscape, leading to improved accuracy and efficiency.
Related Work: Existing work in mixed-precision training and quantization, such as DQT (Dynamic Quantization Training) and SNIP, primarily focus on either dynamic bit-width switching or heuristic-based precision allocation. However, these methods often lack a formal understanding of the relationship between the loss landscape's curvature and the optimal precision configuration. Our proposal builds on these methods by integrating curvature diagnostics to provide a more principled approach to precision routing.
Abstract: We propose Curvature-Aware Adaptive Routing (CAAR), a novel framework for training-free, adaptive mixed-precision neural network training. CAAR leverages curvature diagnostics to dynamically adjust the precision of each layer based on the local geometry of the loss landscape. By integrating spectral quantization and low-overhead implementation, CAAR ensures that precision adjustments are both efficient and theoretically grounded. We demonstrate that CAAR not only improves accuracy but also reduces computational overhead, making it suitable for resource-constrained environments. Our experiments on ResNet and Vision Transformers show significant gains in both accuracy and efficiency compared to existing methods.
Experiments: Conduct a preliminary study to validate the relationship between loss landscape curvature and optimal precision configuration using synthetic datasets and small-scale models like MLPs.
Develop a curvature diagnostic module that computes Hessian conditioning and gradient covariance decay for each layer of a neural network.
Integrate the curvature diagnostic module with a spectral quantization framework to dynamically adjust precision based on the curvature diagnostics.
Evaluate CAAR on standard benchmarks such as CIFAR-10 and ImageNet using ResNet and Vision Transformer architectures.
Compare CAAR's performance with existing mixed-precision methods (e.g., DQT, SNIP, AMP-ViT) in terms of accuracy, computational efficiency, and memory usage.
Perform ablation studies to isolate the impact of curvature diagnostics and spectral quantization on the overall performance.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The computational overhead of curvature diagnostics may be significant, especially for large models and datasets. However, we mitigate this by using efficient spectral quantization and low-overhead implementation.

The curvature diagnostics may not always accurately predict the optimal precision configuration, leading to suboptimal performance. We address this by incorporating adaptive mechanisms and thorough validation.
The proposed framework may not generalize well to all types of neural network architectures and tasks. We will conduct extensive experiments to evaluate its robustness and adaptability.
